symbols: cross — the instrument of salvation and the altar of Christ's sacrifice
crown of thorns — mockery of kingship transformed into the true crown of the King of Kings
INRI inscription in three languages — Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews, proclaiming his identity to all nations
nails and blood — the physical cost of redemption and the blood of the new covenant
white loincloth — purity of the spotless sacrifice amid the shame of crucifixion
utter darkness — the three hours of darkness over the land and the weight of the world's sin
head bowed forward — Christ yielding up his spirit in the moment of death
four nails (feet separate) — traditional Spanish iconography emphasizing the full extension of suffering
solitary figure — the loneliness of the atoning sacrifice, forsaken by all
description: A monumental depiction of Christ crucified against an entirely black background. Christ hangs upon a wooden cross, his body pale and luminous against the darkness. He wears a crown of thorns and a white loincloth. His head droops forward, hair covering part of his face. Blood flows from the nail wounds in his hands and feet, and from his side. Above his head, a plaque bears an inscription in Hebrew, Greek, and Latin. The four nails — one in each hand and one in each foot — are clearly visible.
theological_description: Velazquez's Cristo crucificado is one of the most theologically concentrated Crucifixion images in Western art. The absolute darkness recalls the three hours of supernatural darkness over the land (Mark 15:33), which the Church Fathers interpreted as creation itself mourning its Creator. The solitary figure — no Mary, no John, no thieves — isolates the theological core: this is the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (John 1:29), alone in his atoning work. The INRI inscription in three languages (John 19:19-20) was meant by Pilate as mockery but functions as proclamation — Jesus is King in the language of religion (Hebrew), culture (Greek), and law (Latin). Paul's theology of the cross pervades the image: 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Cor 5:21). The bowed head signals the moment of John 19:30: 'It is finished' — the Greek tetelestai denoting not mere ending but completion, fulfillment, perfection. Isaiah 53 finds its visual fulfillment here: he was pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities; by his wounds we are healed. The four nails reflect Spanish theological tradition emphasizing the full measure of Christ's pain, each wound a point of redemption for the four corners of the earth.

scripture_refs.reason: John's Crucifixion narrative including the inscription, the moment of death, and Christ's declaration 'It is finished'
He was pierced for our transgressions — the Suffering Servant prophecy fulfilled in the crucified body
Darkness over the whole land from the sixth to the ninth hour — the black background embodies this supernatural darkness
Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world — the solitary figure is the sacrificial Lamb
God made him who had no sin to be sin for us — the theological heart of the atoning sacrifice depicted
Christ became a curse for us, for it is written: Cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree
He entered the Most Holy Place once for all by his own blood — the blood flowing from the wounds is the blood of eternal redemption
My God, my God, why have you forsaken me — the utter isolation of the figure evokes Christ's cry of dereliction
